lead_title: ElevenLabs hits $500M ARR with BlackRock, Jamie Foxx, and Eva Longoria backing voice AI platform

options.conviction: L2: Voice isn't a feature you add anymore. It's an interface layer you build your entire product around.
options.post:
ElevenLabs just hit $500M ARR.

$500 million. Not funding. Revenue.

For voice AI infrastructure.

Every team I talk to is still treating voice as a nice-to-have feature. Something you bolt on after you ship the core product.

But BlackRock doesn't invest in nice-to-haves.

They invest in cash flows they can model.

When the world's largest asset manager writes a check for voice AI, that tells you something about where interfaces are going.

Two years ago, voice synthesis cost $0.50 per minute. Took 30 seconds to process.

ElevenLabs pushed that to $0.02 per minute. Real-time.

That price point changed everything.

Voice stopped being a premium feature. Started being default infrastructure.

At Atlan, we've been watching this shift for months. Customer service teams route calls through voice APIs. Training departments generate voice content at scale. Product teams ship voice-first instead of text-only.

The mental shift for builders is huge.

You're not building a product that happens to have voice.

You're building a voice experience that happens to solve a problem.

Applications designed voice-first feel completely different. The user behavior is different. The engagement metrics are different. The retention curves are different.

What does your product feel like when voice is the primary interface?

options.conviction: L1: The enterprise infrastructure layer of AI is generating real revenue while everyone debates features.
options.post:
$500M ARR.

That's what ElevenLabs just posted.

Not venture funding. Not GMV. Recurring revenue.

From enterprise customers paying monthly fees for voice AI infrastructure.

I keep seeing founders debate whether AI features will monetize. Meanwhile, the infrastructure layer already did.

Voice API calls. Real-time synthesis. Enterprise-grade reliability.

The boring stuff nobody talks about at conferences.

BlackRock, Jamie Foxx, and Eva Longoria just backed it. Different reasons, same bet.

The money isn't in the consumer apps using the voice tech.

It's in the pipes the apps can't build themselves.

Two years ago, decent voice cost $0.50 per minute and took 30 seconds to render. ElevenLabs made it $0.02 and real-time.

That's when enterprise budgets opened up.

Teams stopped building voice internally. Started buying voice as infrastructure.

Same pattern we saw with payments (Stripe), communications (Twilio), and auth (Auth0).

The companies that win are the ones that make complex technical capabilities feel like utilities.

Every AI application faces the same choice now: build voice in-house or partner with voice infrastructure.

Building means hiring specialists, maintaining audio pipelines, competing on quality against teams that only do voice.

Partnering means API integration but dependency on external providers.

Most teams choose partnership. That concentrates power in infrastructure companies like ElevenLabs.

The revenue validates what infrastructure players already knew.

Developers will pay premium prices for capabilities they can't easily replicate.

options.conviction: L2: The real story isn't ElevenLabs' $500M—it's that voice AI consolidated around enterprise money while founders chase consumer apps.
options.post:
Everyone's celebrating ElevenLabs' $500M ARR.

I think they're missing the real story.

Voice AI just consolidated around enterprise infrastructure while most founders are still building consumer chat apps.

$500M in recurring revenue doesn't happen by accident. It happens when paying customers have budget line items for your category.

Enterprise teams aren't experimenting with voice anymore. They're operationalizing it.

Customer service workflows route through voice APIs by default. Training content gets generated at voice scale. Product experiences ship voice-first, not text-plus-voice.

The consumer story everyone talks about: "users will chat with AI instead of typing."

The enterprise story nobody talks about: "workflows will speak instead of displaying interfaces."

Two completely different futures.

Consumer apps compete on engagement and retention. Enterprise infrastructure competes on reliability and cost per transaction.

ElevenLabs chose enterprise infrastructure. $0.02 per minute, real-time processing, API-first architecture.

They built the pipes, not the apps.

Most AI founders I know are building the apps, not the pipes.

Chat interfaces for end users. Creative tools for content creators. Productivity features for knowledge workers.

All great markets. But none of them have $500M ARR yet.

The money is flowing to the companies that make everyone else's AI products possible, not the products themselves.

Infrastructure eats features. Every time.

IMO, there's a lesson here for every AI company: ask whether you're building what customers use, or what other builders need to make their customers successful.

Because the builders pay enterprise prices. The customers expect consumer prices.

Which market are you actually in?
